title: Suicide Risk and Safety
subtitle: The nurse keeps a suicidal patient safe — asking directly about suicide, taking a plan with means seriously, never leaving a high-risk patient alone, and removing dangerous items.

patient.name: Suicide Risk / Safety
patient.admitDate: The nurse maintains safety for a patient at risk for suicide.

labs.v: Asking does not plant the idea.
No-harm contract is not a safeguard.

nursesNote: The nurse maintains safety for a patient at risk for suicide. The nurse asks directly and calmly about suicidal thoughts; asking about suicide does not plant the idea and is essential to assess risk. The nurse explores whether the patient has a plan and access to the means to carry it out, since a specific plan with available means (such as a firearm or stockpiled pills) and a history of a prior attempt indicate the highest risk. For a high-risk patient, safety comes first: the patient is placed on close or one-to-one observation and is never left alone, including in the bathroom. The environment is made safe by removing dangerous items such as sharps, glass, cords, belts, and medications. The nurse stays with the patient, listens without judging, and conveys hope while taking every statement seriously, including indirect comments like giving away possessions. A sudden, unexplained calm or improvement in a previously distressed patient can be a warning that a decision to act has been made and warrants increased vigilance. No-harm or no-suicide contracts are not reliable safeguards and do not replace observation and a safe environment. The nurse notifies the provider, documents the risk assessment and interventions, and ensures continuity of safety measures.
providerOrders: Ask the patient directly about suicidal thoughts and a plan
Assess for a plan, access to means, and prior attempts
Place a high-risk patient on close or one-to-one observation
Remove dangerous items and provide a safe environment
Notify the provider and document the risk and interventions

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about suicide risk?

opts.text: Asking directly about suicide is appropriate
A specific plan with means is high risk
A prior attempt raises future risk
Asking about suicide gives the patient the idea
Giving away possessions can be a warning sign
All suicide statements should be taken seriously
A high-risk patient is never left alone
Dangerous items should be removed
A no-harm contract guarantees safety
Access to lethal means raises risk
Sudden calm can be a warning sign
The bathroom is a safe place to leave them alone

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order responding to a patient voicing suicidal thoughts

opts.text: Stay with the patient and ensure they are not alone
Ask directly about a plan and access to means
Remove dangerous items from the area
Notify the provider and arrange observation level
Document the assessment and interventions

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Higher risk or Lower risk indicator?

opts.text: A specific plan with a stockpile of pills
Vague sadness with no plan and future goals
A prior suicide attempt
Strong support system and reasons to live
Access to a firearm at home
Sudden calm after deep despair

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient says 'I have pills at home and a plan to use them tonight'
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Do not leave the patient alone
Ask more about the plan, means, and timing
Initiate one-to-one observation
Notify the provider and team
Document the risk and the safety plan
Asking the patient directly about suicide
Leaving a high-risk patient alone to shower
Removing sharps, cords, and belts
Relying on a no-harm contract for safety
Taking an indirect comment seriously
Dismissing the threat as attention-seeking
Initiating one-to-one observation for high risk
Ignoring a sudden unexplained calm
